processes.name: Grid Fault Management
processes.summary: Safety-critical 3-agent pipeline: SCADA event detection, root cause analysis, and coordinated service restoration with mandatory human gates for every switching operation

feasibility_summary.risk_rationale: Safety-critical infrastructure — agent errors can endanger field workers, damage equipment, or extend outages affecting thousands of customers. Requires mandatory human gates at every switching step.
feasibility_summary.autonomy_note: Detection is fully autonomous; Analysis requires PE review for cascading faults; Restoration requires operator approval for every switching action
feasibility_summary.complexity_rationale: Requires OT/IT integration (SCADA APIs), ML model training on historical fault data, real-time streaming, and load flow simulation integration

breakdown.note: SCADA event context + fault classification prompt + topology query
Fault report + waveform summaries + historical correlation + causal reasoning
RCA context + switching plan generation + safety document templates

key_risks: Safety regulations mandate human approval — cannot fully automate switching
ML fault classifier requires 10,000+ labeled historical events for training
SCADA system integration varies across utility installations
Protection relay firmware compatibility across multi-vendor fleet
key_benefits: 95% reduction in mean time to restoration (30–90 min → ~3 min)
80% alarm reduction during cascading events through intelligent correlation
Consistent response quality independent of individual operator experience
Complete audit trail with reasoning chain for regulatory compliance
Historical correlation surfaces patterns invisible to manual analysis

process_overview.name: Grid Fault Management
process_overview.description: End-to-end fault management from SCADA event detection through root cause analysis to coordinated service restoration. Currently a 30–90 minute manual process involving grid operators, protection engineers, and field service engineers coordinating via radio and phone. The target state uses three specialized AI agents to reduce mean time to restoration to approximately 3 minutes.

current_timeline.description: Operator notices alarm cascade on SCADA dashboard, manually correlates protection relay events across multiple substations
Protection engineer reviews disturbance recordings, traces fault propagation through grid topology, identifies root cause and affected equipment
Grid operator plans switching sequence, coordinates with field crews and neighboring operators, executes step-by-step with safety verification

actors.name: Grid Operator
actors.involvement: Monitors SCADA dashboards, manually correlates alarms, approves all switching operations, coordinates with field crews and neighboring operators

actors.name: Protection Engineer
actors.involvement: Reviews disturbance recordings, performs root cause analysis for complex faults, validates protection relay operations

actors.name: Field Service Engineer
actors.involvement: Performs physical switching operations at substations, confirms safety isolation, reports step completion to control room

systems.name: Network Manager ADMS
systems.role: Advanced distribution management — load flow simulation and automated switching capabilities

systems.role: Asset performance management — historical condition data, DGA trends, maintenance records

data_objects.name: Fault Report
data_objects.description: Created at detection, enriched through analysis and restoration — becomes the permanent incident record

data_objects.name: Grid Asset
data_objects.description: Equipment data — ratings, condition history, location — used for impact assessment and restoration path planning

data_objects.name: Fault History

pain_points.title: Alarm Flooding
pain_points.description: A single grid fault can generate 200+ alarms within seconds, overwhelming operators and obscuring the root cause signal in noise

pain_points.title: Manual Topology Tracing
pain_points.description: Operators must mentally trace fault propagation through complex grid topology — error-prone under time pressure, especially during cascading events

pain_points.title: Sequential Coordination
pain_points.description: Detection, analysis, and restoration happen sequentially with phone/radio handoffs between control room, protection engineers, and field crews

pain_points.title: Inconsistent Response Quality
pain_points.description: Response effectiveness depends heavily on individual operator experience — a 20-year veteran handles cascading faults differently than a new operator

pain_points.title: Safety Documentation Gap
pain_points.description: Switching operations require manual safety documentation that slows restoration but exists for good reason — protecting field workers from energized equipment

agent_steps.name: Fault Detection & Isolation
agent_steps.autonomy_description: Operates autonomously — no human approval needed for detection and classification. Outputs are informational, not actuating.
agent_steps.summary: Ingests raw SCADA events and protection relay data in real-time, applies ML fault classification to identify fault type and location, filters alarm noise, and produces a structured Fault Report for downstream agents.

context_data.description: Protection relay trip signals, breaker status changes, voltage/current measurements from RTUs across the distribution network
Real-time network connectivity model — which breakers are open/closed, which feeders are energized, substation bus configurations
Labeled historical fault events with confirmed root causes — used for ML model training and pattern matching (10,000+ events over 5 years)
Relay settings, protection zones, coordination curves — defines expected relay behavior for valid fault scenarios

skills.name: Fault Classification
skills.description: ML model trained on historical fault signatures classifies fault type (single-line-to-ground, line-to-line, three-phase) and estimates location using impedance-based and travelling-wave methods

skills.name: Alarm Correlation
skills.description: Groups related alarms using temporal proximity, electrical connectivity, and protection zone relationships — reduces 200+ raw alarms to 3–5 correlated alarm groups

skills.name: Topology Analysis
skills.description: Traces grid connectivity from fault point to identify all affected feeders, substations, and customers. Uses real-time breaker states from SCADA.

skills.name: Anomaly Filtering
skills.description: Distinguishes genuine fault events from sensor noise, communication errors, and planned maintenance activities using confidence scoring

tools_and_data.purpose: Subscribe to real-time SCADA event stream
Query real-time grid topology and connectivity
ML inference for fault type and location
Read disturbance recordings from IEDs

guardrails.description: Detection Agent can read SCADA data and classify faults but CANNOT issue any switching commands, breaker trips, or control actions
Fault classification must meet 95% confidence threshold before generating a Fault Report. Below threshold, alert operator for manual review
If Detection Agent fails or times out (>30s), all raw alarms are forwarded to grid operator dashboard with no filtering — never suppress alarms

outputs.name: Fault Report
outputs.description: Contains: fault type, estimated location (substation + feeder), confidence score, affected customer count, correlated alarm groups, timestamp

outputs.name: Operator Notification
outputs.description: Real-time notification to grid operator dashboard showing fault summary with option to override or escalate

involved_roles.involvement: Receives notification. Can override classification or escalate. No approval needed for detection itself.

agent_steps.name: Fault Analysis & Root Cause
agent_steps.autonomy_description: Performs analysis autonomously but root cause determination for complex or cascading faults requires Protection Engineer confirmation before proceeding to restoration.
agent_steps.summary: Receives the Fault Report from the Detection Agent and performs deep analysis: graph traversal of grid topology to trace fault propagation, waveform analysis of disturbance recordings, historical correlation with similar past events, and equipment health assessment. Produces a Root Cause Analysis with confidence-weighted recommendations.

context_data.description: Structured fault classification including type, location estimate, confidence score, and correlated alarm groups from Step 1
Full electrical connectivity model with real-time switching state — used for fault propagation tracing and isolation zone identification
Historical condition data for affected equipment — DGA trends, thermal history, maintenance records, age, and remaining useful life estimates
High-resolution voltage and current waveforms captured by protection relays at fault inception — critical for determining fault characteristics
Current and recent weather conditions at fault location — lightning strikes, wind speed, temperature for environmental correlation

skills.name: Graph-Based Fault Tracing
skills.description: Traverses the grid topology graph from the detected fault point, following electrical connectivity through switches and transformers to identify all affected zones, isolation boundaries, and potential restoration paths

skills.name: Waveform Analysis
skills.description: Analyzes disturbance recording waveforms to extract fault inception angle, fault resistance, and arc characteristics — distinguishes permanent faults from transient events

skills.name: Historical Correlation
skills.description: Matches current fault signature against historical database to find similar past events, their confirmed root causes, and restoration outcomes — provides evidence-based confidence scoring

skills.name: Equipment Impact Assessment
skills.description: Evaluates the health and stress impact on affected equipment using Lumada APM data — flags equipment that may be damaged or at risk of secondary failure

skills.name: Causal Chain Reasoning
skills.description: Constructs a causal chain from triggering event through protection operations to observed consequences — identifies whether relay operations were correct or if protection mis-coordination occurred

tools_and_data.purpose: Graph traversal of grid topology model
Query asset condition and health scores
Retrieve fault waveform data from IEDs
Neo4j-based grid connectivity model for path analysis
Pattern match against 10,000+ labeled historical events

guardrails.description: If fault affects more than 3 substations or 50,000 customers, the root cause analysis MUST be reviewed by a Protection Engineer before proceeding to restoration planning
Root cause confidence score must be prominently displayed. If confidence < 80%, analysis must present multiple hypotheses ranked by probability rather than a single determination
If analysis indicates potential equipment damage (transformer winding fault, cable damage), flag for physical inspection before re-energization — do not include damaged equipment in restoration path

outputs.name: Root Cause Analysis
outputs.description: Contains: confirmed root cause, causal chain, affected equipment list, damage assessment, confidence score, recommended restoration approach, historical precedents cited

outputs.description: If equipment damage detected: affected asset IDs, damage type, inspection requirements, estimated repair time. Blocks automatic re-energization of damaged paths.

involved_roles.involvement: Reviews and confirms root cause analysis for cascading faults or low-confidence results. Approves causal chain determination.
Monitors analysis progress on dashboard. Can request expedited analysis or override if situation changes.

agent_steps.name: Service Restoration
agent_steps.autonomy_description: Generates restoration plan autonomously but every switching action requires explicit Grid Operator approval. Field crew dispatch requires Field Service Engineer acceptance.
agent_steps.summary: Receives the Root Cause Analysis and generates an optimal restoration plan: identifies switching sequences to restore maximum load while respecting equipment ratings, generates safety isolation documents, simulates the restoration plan against a load flow model, and dispatches field crews with mobile work orders for physical switching operations.

context_data.description: Confirmed root cause, affected equipment, damage flags, and recommended restoration approach from Step 2
Advanced distribution management system with load flow simulation, real-time load data, and automated switching capabilities
Real-time crew locations, qualifications, availability, and current assignments — used for optimal crew dispatch
Customer criticality classifications (hospitals, water treatment, data centers) for load shedding decisions
Thermal ratings, switching limits, and operational constraints for all grid equipment — prevents overloading during restoration

skills.name: Switching Sequence Optimization
skills.description: Generates optimal switching sequence to restore maximum load with minimum operations — considers equipment ratings, protection coordination, and isolation requirements

skills.name: Load Flow Simulation
skills.description: Runs power flow simulation of proposed restoration plan to verify voltage profiles, thermal loading, and stability before execution — rejects plans that violate constraints

skills.name: Safety Document Generation
skills.description: Auto-generates switching programs, safety isolation certificates, and work permits with all required fields — follows utility-specific safety documentation standards

skills.name: Crew Dispatch Optimization
skills.description: Selects optimal field crew based on location, qualifications, and current workload — generates mobile work order with step-by-step instructions and GPS routing

skills.name: Load Priority Management
skills.description: When full restoration is not immediately possible, prioritizes load restoration based on customer criticality — hospitals and critical infrastructure first

tools_and_data.purpose: Load flow simulation and switching execution
Execute approved switching commands via SCADA
Dispatch work orders to field crews
Create maintenance work orders for damaged equipment
Power system simulation for plan validation

guardrails.description: Every switching action in the restoration sequence requires explicit Grid Operator approval via the SCADA HMI. Agent proposes, operator disposes. No autonomous switching.
Restoration plan MUST pass load flow simulation before presentation to operator. Plans that cause overloading, voltage violations, or stability issues are rejected and re-optimized.
No field crew dispatch until safety isolation documents are generated, reviewed, and the isolation switching sequence is completed and confirmed via SCADA.
Before energizing any section, agent verifies absence of voltage via SCADA measurements and requires field crew confirmation of earthing removal where applicable.

orchestration.description: The three agents operate in a pipeline orchestration pattern triggered by a real-time SCADA event. Each agent produces a structured output that becomes the primary input for the next agent. Human oversight increases at each stage — from no approval needed for detection to step-by-step approval for restoration.

shared_state.name: Fault Report Lifecycle
shared_state.description: A single Fault Report object (e-data-005) is created by the Detection Agent and enriched by each subsequent agent. It serves as the shared state across the pipeline and becomes the permanent record for regulatory and learning purposes.

Root cause, causal chain, equipment assessment, damage flags
Switching sequence, simulation results, safety documents, crew assignments
Completion timestamp, actual restoration time, operator modifications, lessons learned

guardrails_framework.description: Grid fault management is safety-critical infrastructure — guardrails are not optional compliance add-ons but fundamental operational constraints. The framework defines four priority levels and four mandatory human gates.

levels.label: Life Safety
levels.description: Protects human life — field workers, operators, and the public. These guardrails are absolute and cannot be overridden by any agent or optimization objective.
rules: No autonomous switching — every switching action requires explicit operator approval
Safety isolation documents must be generated and confirmed before any field crew dispatch
Dead line verification required before energizing any previously faulted section
Detection Agent cannot issue any control commands — read-only access to SCADA

levels.label: Equipment Protection
levels.description: Prevents equipment damage that could extend outages, cause environmental contamination (transformer oil), or create secondary safety hazards.
rules: Load flow simulation must validate every restoration plan before presentation to operator
Equipment flagged as potentially damaged is excluded from restoration paths until physically inspected
Thermal ratings and switching duty limits must not be exceeded in any proposed restoration sequence
Detection Agent failsafe: if agent is unresponsive >30s, forward all raw alarms unfiltered to operator

levels.label: Operational Quality
levels.description: Ensures operational decisions are well-founded and auditable. Prevents agents from acting on uncertain or incomplete information.
rules: Fault classification requires ≥95% confidence before generating Fault Report
Root cause analysis with <80% confidence must present multiple ranked hypotheses
All agent decisions must be logged with reasoning chain for post-incident review
Restoration plan must include rollback procedures for each switching step

levels.label: Regulatory Compliance
levels.description: Ensures compliance with grid code, safety regulations, and utility operating procedures. Maintains audit trail for regulatory reporting.
rules: All switching operations recorded per grid code requirements
Outage duration and affected customers tracked for regulatory KPI reporting
Post-incident report auto-generated for regulatory submission
Inter-utility coordination protocols followed for boundary faults

human_gates.trigger: Fault affects >3 substations or >50,000 customers
human_gates.description: Protection Engineer must review and confirm root cause analysis before restoration planning can begin

human_gates.trigger: Every switching operation in restoration sequence
human_gates.description: Grid Operator explicitly approves, modifies, or rejects each proposed switching action

human_gates.trigger: Physical switching operation required at site
human_gates.description: Field Service Engineer accepts work order, confirms safety isolation, and reports step completion

human_gates.trigger: Root cause confidence < 80%
human_gates.description: Protection Engineer selects most likely root cause from ranked hypotheses presented by Analysis Agent

implementation.name: FaultReport
implementation.description: Shared state object passed between all three agents. Created by the Detection Agent and progressively enriched through Analysis and Restoration. Becomes the permanent incident record on resolution.
implementation.code:
from __future__ import annotations
from datetime import datetime
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


class FaultType(str, Enum):
    SLG = "SLG"          # Single-line-to-ground
    LL = "LL"            # Line-to-line
    LLG = "LLG"          # Line-to-line-to-ground
    THREE_PHASE = "THREE_PHASE"


class FaultStatus(str, Enum):
    CREATED = "CREATED"
    ANALYZED = "ANALYZED"
    RESTORATION_PLANNED = "RESTORATION_PLANNED"
    RESOLVED = "RESOLVED"


class SubstationRef(BaseModel):
    id: str
    name: str
    feeder_id: str
    voltage_level_kv: float
    coordinates: tuple[float, float]


class FaultReport(BaseModel):
    """Shared state for the Grid Fault Management pipeline."""
    id: str = Field(description="Unique incident ID, e.g. FLT-2024-00147")
    fault_type: FaultType
    location: SubstationRef
    confidence: float = Field(ge=0.0, le=1.0)
    affected_customers: int
    correlated_alarms: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    status: FaultStatus = FaultStatus.CREATED
    root_cause: Optional[str] = None
    damage_flags: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    created_at: datetime = Field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)
    analyzed_at: Optional[datetime] = None
    restored_at: Optional[datetime] = None

implementation.description: Represents a single switching operation in the restoration plan. Every step must pass load flow validation and receive explicit operator approval before execution.

from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


class SwitchAction(str, Enum):

    CLOSE = "CLOSE"


class SwitchingStep(BaseModel):

implementation.name: classify_fault
implementation.description: Applies an ML fault classifier to a burst of SCADA events to determine fault type and estimated location. Returns a structured classification with confidence score. Below the 95% threshold the result is flagged for operator review rather than triggering downstream agents.
implementation.code:
from __future__ import annotations
import asyncio
from dataclasses import dataclass

from .models import FaultType, SubstationRef
from .clients import FaultClassifierClient, GridTopologyClient


@dataclass
class FaultClassification:
    fault_type: FaultType
    location: SubstationRef
    confidence: float
    affected_customers: int
    correlated_alarm_ids: list[str]
    raw_event_ids: list[str]


async def classify_fault(
    scada_events: list[dict],
    topology: GridTopologyClient,
    classifier: FaultClassifierClient,
) -> FaultClassification:
    """Classify a burst of SCADA protection events into a typed fault."""
    # Extract feature vector from raw SCADA event burst
    features = classifier.extract_features(scada_events)

    # Run ML inference (ResNet-based time-series classifier)
    prediction = await classifier.infer_async(features)

    # Resolve location from impedance estimate + topology
    location = await topology.resolve_substation(
        zone_id=prediction.zone_id,
        feeder_hint=prediction.feeder_hint,
    )

    affected = await topology.estimate_affected_customers(location)

    return FaultClassification(
        fault_type=FaultType(prediction.label),
        location=location,
        confidence=prediction.confidence,
        affected_customers=affected,
        correlated_alarm_ids=prediction.correlated_ids,
        raw_event_ids=[e["id"] for e in scada_events],
    )

implementation.name: trace_fault_propagation
implementation.description: Traverses the live grid topology graph via BFS starting from the detected fault point. Identifies all affected zones, isolation boundaries, and candidate restoration paths. Returns the set of de-energized zones and the nearest upstream isolation points.
implementation.code:
from __future__ import annotations
from collections import deque
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

from .models import SubstationRef
from .clients import GridTopologyClient


@dataclass
class PropagationResult:
    fault_origin: SubstationRef
    affected_zone_ids: list[str]
    isolation_boundary: list[str]   # Equipment IDs of nearest open/trip points
    de_energized_feeders: list[str]
    restoration_paths: list[list[str]]  # Candidate alternate feed paths
    substation_count: int


async def trace_fault_propagation(
    origin: SubstationRef,
    topology: GridTopologyClient,
) -> PropagationResult:
    """BFS traversal of grid topology from fault origin to identify impact zone."""
    visited: set[str] = set()
    boundary: list[str] = []
    affected: list[str] = []
    queue: deque[str] = deque([origin.id])

    while queue:
        node_id = queue.popleft()
        if node_id in visited:
            continue
        visited.add(node_id)
        node = await topology.get_node(node_id)

        if node.is_isolated or node.breaker_open:
            boundary.append(node_id)
            continue

        affected.append(node_id)
        neighbours = await topology.get_energised_neighbours(node_id)
        queue.extend(n for n in neighbours if n not in visited)

    paths = await topology.find_alternate_paths(origin.id, boundary)
    return PropagationResult(
        fault_origin=origin,
        affected_zone_ids=affected,
        isolation_boundary=boundary,
        de_energized_feeders=await topology.get_feeder_ids(affected),
        restoration_paths=paths,
        substation_count=len([n for n in affected if topology.is_substation(n)]),
    )

implementation.name: generate_restoration_plan
implementation.description: Generates an optimal switching sequence to restore maximum load after a confirmed fault. Runs load flow simulation on each candidate plan and rejects sequences that violate voltage or thermal constraints. Returns a validated RestorationPlan with operator-facing switching steps.
implementation.code:
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass

from .models import SwitchingStep, SwitchAction
from .clients import ADMSClient, LoadFlowEngine


@dataclass
class RestorationPlan:
    steps: list[SwitchingStep]
    estimated_restoration_time_min: int
    customers_restored: int
    load_flow_passed: bool
    load_shedding_required: bool
    violated_constraints: list[str]


async def generate_restoration_plan(
    root_cause: dict,
    grid_state: dict,
    adms: ADMSClient,
    load_flow: LoadFlowEngine,
) -> RestorationPlan:
    """Generate and validate an optimal switching sequence for service restoration."""
    # Get candidate switching sequences from ADMS optimiser
    candidates = await adms.get_switching_candidates(
        affected_zones=root_cause["affected_zone_ids"],
        restoration_paths=root_cause["restoration_paths"],
        priority_loads=grid_state["critical_customer_ids"],
    )

    for candidate in candidates:
        # Run power flow simulation on each candidate
        lf_result = await load_flow.simulate(candidate.switching_ops, grid_state)

        if lf_result.all_constraints_met:
            steps = [
                SwitchingStep(
                    sequence_number=i + 1,
                    action=SwitchAction(op["action"]),
                    equipment_id=op["equipment_id"],
                    equipment_name=op["equipment_name"],
                    substation=op["substation"],
                    requires_field_crew=op.get("field_crew_required", False),
                    load_flow_validated=True,
                )
                for i, op in enumerate(candidate.switching_ops)
            ]
            return RestorationPlan(
                steps=steps,
                estimated_restoration_time_min=len(steps) * 3,
                customers_restored=lf_result.customers_restored,
                load_flow_passed=True,
                load_shedding_required=lf_result.load_shedding_applied,
                violated_constraints=[],
            )

    raise ValueError("No feasible restoration plan found — escalate to grid operator")

implementation.name: NoActuationGuard
implementation.description: Blocks any SCADA write or control operation attempted by the Detection Agent. The Detection Agent operates in a strictly read-only mode — it may subscribe to event streams and read topology data, but cannot issue switching commands, breaker trips, or any other control action.

implementation.name: ConfidenceThresholdGuard
implementation.description: Ensures fault classification confidence meets the 95% threshold before a Fault Report is issued and dispatched to the Analysis Agent. Below threshold, the grid operator is alerted for manual review rather than triggering downstream automation.

implementation.name: CascadingFaultGate
implementation.description: Detects cascading fault conditions (more than 3 substations or more than 50,000 customers affected) and halts automatic progression to restoration planning until a Protection Engineer has reviewed and confirmed the root cause analysis.
implementation.code:
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal
import asyncio

from .clients import ApprovalGateway


@dataclass
class GuardrailResult:
    allowed: bool
    reason: str
    action: Literal["proceed", "block", "escalate"]
    pending_approver: str | None = None


CASCADING_SUBSTATION_THRESHOLD = 3
CASCADING_CUSTOMER_THRESHOLD = 50_000


class CascadingFaultGate:
    """Life-safety guardrail: cascading faults require Protection Engineer review."""

    def __init__(self, approval_gateway: ApprovalGateway):
        self.gateway = approval_gateway

    async def check(self, context: dict) -> GuardrailResult:
        propagation = context.get("propagation_result", {})
        substation_count = propagation.get("substation_count", 0)
        affected_customers = propagation.get("affected_customers", 0)

        is_cascading = (
            substation_count > CASCADING_SUBSTATION_THRESHOLD
            or affected_customers > CASCADING_CUSTOMER_THRESHOLD
        )

        if is_cascading:
            # Request async approval — blocks pipeline until PE confirms
            await self.gateway.request_approval(
                role="protection_engineer",
                context=context,
                message=(
                    f"Cascading fault detected: {substation_count} substations, "
                    f"{affected_customers:,} customers affected. "
                    "Review RCA before restoration planning proceeds."
                ),
            )
            return GuardrailResult(
                allowed=False,
                reason="Cascading fault — Protection Engineer approval required",
                action="escalate",
                pending_approver="protection_engineer",
            )
        return GuardrailResult(allowed=True, reason="Contained fault — proceeding", action="proceed")

implementation.name: OperatorApprovalGuard
implementation.description: Enforces the mandatory operator approval gate for every switching step in the restoration sequence. Presents the proposed action to the Grid Operator via SCADA HMI and waits for explicit approval, modification, or rejection before the step is executed.

implementation.name: LoadFlowValidationGuard
implementation.description: Runs power flow simulation on the full restoration plan before it is presented to the operator. Rejects any plan where voltage profiles, thermal loading, or stability margins violate network constraints. Triggers re-optimisation rather than silent failure.
implementation.code:
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

from .clients import LoadFlowEngine
from .models import SwitchingStep


@dataclass
class GuardrailResult:
    allowed: bool
    reason: str
    action: Literal["proceed", "block", "reoptimise"]
    violations: list[str] | None = None


class LoadFlowValidationGuard:
    """Equipment-protection guardrail: restoration plan must pass load flow simulation."""

    def __init__(self, engine: LoadFlowEngine):
        self.engine = engine

    async def check(self, context: dict) -> GuardrailResult:
        steps: list[SwitchingStep] = context["restoration_steps"]
        grid_state: dict = context["grid_state"]

        result = await self.engine.simulate(
            switching_ops=[{"equipment_id": s.equipment_id, "action": s.action} for s in steps],
            base_state=grid_state,
        )

        if result.all_constraints_met:
            return GuardrailResult(
                allowed=True,
                reason="Load flow validated — no voltage or thermal violations",
                action="proceed",
            )

        violations = [
            f"{v.equipment_id}: {v.type} violation ({v.value:.1f} vs limit {v.limit:.1f})"
            for v in result.violations
        ]
        return GuardrailResult(
            allowed=False,
            reason=f"Load flow failed: {len(violations)} constraint violation(s)",
            action="reoptimise",
            violations=violations,
        )

processes.name: Customer Service Request
processes.summary: Autonomous email processing: parse multi-order delivery status inquiry, look up each order across CRM and logistics, compose personalized response — currently 10–15 min manual, target < 30 seconds

feasibility_summary.risk_rationale: Non-safety-critical service process. Worst-case agent error is a wrong tracking number in an email — easily corrected, no physical or financial harm. Data privacy is the primary concern.
feasibility_summary.autonomy_note: 85% of status inquiries are routine and handled end-to-end without human involvement. CSR review only for Tier 1 customers, delayed orders with no ETA, or ambiguous requests.
feasibility_summary.complexity_rationale: Salesforce and SAP APIs are well-documented; email parsing uses standard NLP; main challenge is mapping diverse order reference formats and handling multi-language responses

breakdown.note: Email body + customer context + intent classification + order extraction
Order data aggregation across Salesforce + SAP TM + S/4HANA (mostly API, some reasoning)
Status data + template selection + personalized email composition in customer language

key_risks: Data privacy — must verify customer identity before sharing order information
Hallucination risk — agent must never fabricate tracking numbers or delivery dates
Multi-language quality — response quality must match human CSR across 6 languages
Edge case routing — must reliably detect complaints vs. routine inquiries
key_benefits: 97% reduction in response time (10–15 min → <30s for routine requests)
Zero copy-paste errors — tracking numbers come directly from system queries
24/7 availability — status inquiries answered outside business hours
CSR capacity freed for high-value activities (complaints, complex issues, relationship management)
Proactive delay notifications become feasible at near-zero marginal cost

process_overview.name: Customer Service Request
process_overview.description: A customer contacts the service center — typically via email — with a request such as checking delivery status for multiple orders. A Customer Service Representative reads the email, extracts order references, searches the CRM (Salesforce Sales Cloud) and logistics system (SAP TM) for each order, collects tracking numbers and delivery dates, and composes a response email with the consolidated information. Currently takes 10–15 minutes per request, with higher-complexity requests (multiple products, cross-system lookups) taking longer. The target state uses an AI agent that handles the full cycle autonomously for routine status inquiries, escalating only when human judgment is truly needed.

CSR composes a reply email listing each order with its status and tracking number, re-reads for accuracy, and sends — attaches the case to the customer record

pain_points.description: Tracking numbers and order statuses are manually copied between systems — transposition errors in 16-digit tracking numbers lead to frustrated customers and follow-up requests

pain_points.title: Volume Bottleneck
pain_points.description: Each CSR handles ~30 requests per day. During peak periods (product launches, holiday season), email queue backlog grows to 4–6 hour response times

guardrails_framework.description: Customer service guardrails protect data privacy, ensure response accuracy, and maintain service quality. Unlike safety-critical processes, these guardrails primarily govern information access and communication quality rather than physical safety.

levels.label: Data Privacy
levels.description: Protects customer personal data and ensures order information is only shared with the verified account holder. GDPR and data protection compliance.
rules: Customer identity must be verified against CRM before any order data is retrieved
Agent may only access orders belonging to the authenticated customer — no cross-account queries
Customer PII (address, payment details) is never included in status response emails
All data access is logged for compliance audit trail

levels.label: Information Accuracy
levels.description: Ensures every piece of information in the customer response is sourced from enterprise systems of record. Prevents hallucination, estimation, or guessing.
rules: Tracking numbers, dates, and statuses must come from system queries — never estimated or fabricated
If an order cannot be found, explicitly state this rather than omitting or guessing
Delivery date estimates use carrier-provided ETAs, not agent predictions
Response must not contain information beyond what was retrieved in the data gathering step

levels.label: Service Quality
levels.description: Maintains the expected quality of customer communication. Ensures complex or sensitive situations get human attention.
rules: Tier 1 customers always get CSR review before response is sent
Delayed orders with no ETA require CSR review to set realistic expectations
Complaints, returns, and ambiguous requests are routed to human CSR queue
Response must acknowledge every order the customer asked about — even if status is unknown

levels.label: Brand & Tone
levels.description: Ensures all automated communication aligns with company brand voice and communication standards.
rules: Professional but warm tone — no jargon, no overpromising
Carrier-neutral language when discussing shipping delays
Response in customer's preferred language using approved templates
Company email signature and formatting standards applied

human_gates.trigger: Request classification confidence < 90% or type is complaint/return
human_gates.description: CSR takes over for requests the agent cannot confidently classify as routine status inquiries

human_gates.trigger: Customer is Tier 1 (top 50 accounts)
human_gates.description: CSR reviews draft response before sending to ensure it meets the relationship expectations of key accounts

human_gates.trigger: Order delayed with no confirmed new ETA
human_gates.description: CSR reviews and enriches response with internal context about the delay and commits to a follow-up timeline

implementation.name: ServiceCase
implementation.description: Shared state object created by the Intake Agent and progressively enriched through Retrieval and Response steps. Becomes the permanent Salesforce Service Case record on resolution.
implementation.code:
from __future__ import annotations
from datetime import datetime
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


class RequestType(str, Enum):
    STATUS_INQUIRY = "STATUS_INQUIRY"
    COMPLAINT = "COMPLAINT"
    RETURN = "RETURN"
    TECHNICAL_SUPPORT = "TECHNICAL_SUPPORT"
    BILLING = "BILLING"
    OTHER = "OTHER"


class CaseStatus(str, Enum):
    CREATED = "CREATED"
    ENRICHED = "ENRICHED"
    RESOLVED = "RESOLVED"


class HandlingMode(str, Enum):
    AUTO = "AUTO"
    REVIEWED = "REVIEWED"


class ServiceCase(BaseModel):
    """Shared state object for the Customer Service Request pipeline."""
    id: str = Field(description="Salesforce Case ID, e.g. CASE-2024-08471")
    customer_id: str
    customer_tier: int = Field(ge=1, le=3, description="1=Enterprise, 2=Commercial, 3=Standard")
    request_type: RequestType
    extracted_orders: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    status: CaseStatus = CaseStatus.CREATED
    order_statuses: list[dict] = Field(default_factory=list)
    response_draft: Optional[str] = None
    handling_mode: HandlingMode = HandlingMode.AUTO
    original_email_ref: str = Field(description="Message-ID of inbound email")
    created_at: datetime = Field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)
    resolved_at: Optional[datetime] = None

from typing import Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


class OrderStatus(BaseModel):

implementation.name: parse_email
implementation.description: Uses an LLM with structured output to extract intent, customer identity signals, and all order references from the raw inbound email. Returns a structured ParsedEmail with classification confidence, extracted order numbers, and detected urgency signals.
implementation.code:
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

from .clients import LLMClient, CRMClient
from .models import RequestType


@dataclass
class ParsedEmail:
    sender_email: str
    customer_id: str | None
    customer_name: str | None
    request_type: RequestType
    classification_confidence: float
    extracted_order_numbers: list[str]
    detected_language: str
    urgency_score: float   # 0.0 – 1.0
    raw_subject: str
    raw_body: str


EXTRACTION_PROMPT = """
Extract from the email:
1. request_type: one of STATUS_INQUIRY, COMPLAINT, RETURN, TECHNICAL_SUPPORT, BILLING, OTHER
2. confidence: 0.0–1.0
3. order_numbers: all order/PO references mentioned (any format)
4. urgency_score: 0.0–1.0 based on language tone
5. detected_language: ISO 639-1 code
Respond as JSON.
"""


async def parse_email(
    raw_email: dict,
    llm: LLMClient,
    crm: CRMClient,
) -> ParsedEmail:
    """Parse and classify an inbound customer email using LLM structured output."""
    body = raw_email.get("body", "")
    sender = raw_email.get("from", "")

    # LLM extraction with structured JSON output
    extracted = await llm.complete_json(
        system=EXTRACTION_PROMPT,
        user=f"Subject: {raw_email['subject']}\n\n{body}",
    )

    # Match sender to CRM contact
    customer = await crm.find_contact_by_email(sender)

    return ParsedEmail(
        sender_email=sender,
        customer_id=customer.id if customer else None,
        customer_name=customer.name if customer else None,
        request_type=RequestType(extracted["request_type"]),
        classification_confidence=extracted["confidence"],
        extracted_order_numbers=extracted["order_numbers"],
        detected_language=extracted["detected_language"],
        urgency_score=extracted["urgency_score"],
        raw_subject=raw_email["subject"],
        raw_body=body,
    )

implementation.name: retrieve_order_status
implementation.description: Queries Salesforce, SAP TM, and SAP S/4HANA in parallel for each order number extracted by the Intake Agent. Normalises status codes across all three systems into a unified customer-facing view. Detects delays against promised delivery dates and flags split deliveries.
implementation.code:
from __future__ import annotations
import asyncio
from datetime import date

from .models import OrderStatus
from .clients import SalesforceClient, SAPTMClient, SAPS4Client


STATUS_MAP = {
    "Shipped": "On its way",
    "In Transit": "On its way",
    "GOODS_ISSUED": "Dispatched from warehouse",
    "Delivered": "Delivered",
    "In Production": "In production",
}


async def retrieve_order_status(
    order_numbers: list[str],
    customer_id: str,
    sf: SalesforceClient,
    sap_tm: SAPTMClient,
    sap_s4: SAPS4Client,
) -> list[OrderStatus]:
    """Query CRM and logistics systems in parallel; normalise into per-order status view."""
    async def fetch_single(order_number: str) -> OrderStatus:
        # Fire all three queries concurrently
        sf_order, tm_shipment, s4_prod = await asyncio.gather(
            sf.get_order(order_number, customer_id=customer_id),
            sap_tm.get_shipment(order_number),
            sap_s4.get_production_status(order_number),
            return_exceptions=True,
        )

        if isinstance(sf_order, Exception):
            return OrderStatus(order_number=order_number, found=False, status="Not found")

        raw_status = tm_shipment.status if not isinstance(tm_shipment, Exception) else sf_order.status
        friendly_status = STATUS_MAP.get(raw_status, raw_status)

        eta = (
            tm_shipment.estimated_delivery
            if not isinstance(tm_shipment, Exception)
            else sf_order.expected_delivery_date
        )

        is_delayed = bool(eta and eta < date.today() and raw_status != "Delivered")

        return OrderStatus(
            order_number=order_number,
            found=True,
            products=sf_order.line_items,
            quantity=sf_order.total_quantity,
            status=friendly_status,
            carrier=getattr(tm_shipment, "carrier", None),
            tracking_number=getattr(tm_shipment, "tracking_number", None),
            estimated_delivery=eta,
            is_delayed=is_delayed,
            delay_reason=getattr(tm_shipment, "delay_reason", None),
            is_split_delivery=getattr(tm_shipment, "is_split", False),
        )

    return await asyncio.gather(*[fetch_single(o) for o in order_numbers])

implementation.name: compose_response
implementation.description: Uses an LLM to generate a personalised, professional response email in the customer's preferred language. Selects the appropriate template (standard status, delay notification, split delivery) and populates it with the retrieved order statuses. Returns the HTML draft for guardrail review before dispatch.
implementation.code:
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass

from .models import OrderStatus, ServiceCase
from .clients import LLMClient, TemplateEngine, CRMClient


@dataclass
class ComposedResponse:
    subject: str
    html_body: str
    plain_text_body: str
    detected_tone: str
    template_used: str
    language: str


COMPOSE_SYSTEM = """
You are a professional customer service representative for an industrial equipment company.
Write a {tone} response in {language} using ONLY the order data provided.
NEVER fabricate tracking numbers, dates, or statuses not present in the data.
Structure: greeting, one paragraph per order, closing with offered follow-up.
"""


async def compose_response(
    case: ServiceCase,
    order_statuses: list[OrderStatus],
    crm: CRMClient,
    llm: LLMClient,
    templates: TemplateEngine,
) -> ComposedResponse:
    """Compose personalised response email grounded in retrieved order data only."""
    customer = await crm.get_customer(case.customer_id)

    has_delays = any(o.is_delayed for o in order_statuses)
    has_splits = any(o.is_split_delivery for o in order_statuses)
    template_key = "delay_notification" if has_delays else "split_delivery" if has_splits else "status_update"
    template = await templates.load(template_key, language=customer.preferred_language)

    tone = "empathetic" if has_delays else "professional"
    order_data_block = "\n".join(
        f"Order {o.order_number}: {o.status}, carrier {o.carrier}, tracking {o.tracking_number}, ETA {o.estimated_delivery}"
        for o in order_statuses
    )

    html_body = await llm.complete(
        system=COMPOSE_SYSTEM.format(tone=tone, language=customer.preferred_language),
        user=f"Customer: {customer.name}\nOrders:\n{order_data_block}\nTemplate hint: {template.hint}",
    )

    return ComposedResponse(
        subject=f"Re: Your order status — {case.id}",
        html_body=html_body,
        plain_text_body=llm.html_to_plain(html_body),
        detected_tone=tone,
        template_used=template_key,
        language=customer.preferred_language,
    )

implementation.name: CustomerIdentityGuard
implementation.description: Verifies that the sender email address is associated with a known CRM contact before any order data is retrieved. Blocks processing and routes to human CSR queue if the sender cannot be matched, preventing cross-account data exposure.

implementation.name: NoFabricationGuard
implementation.description: Validates that every tracking number, delivery date, and order status in the composed response draft can be found verbatim in the retrieved OrderStatus data. Blocks dispatch and triggers re-composition if any ungrounded facts are detected.
implementation.code:
from __future__ import annotations
import re
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

from .models import OrderStatus


@dataclass
class GuardrailResult:
    allowed: bool
    reason: str
    action: Literal["proceed", "block", "recompose"]
    ungrounded_facts: list[str] | None = None


# Pattern for tracking numbers (alphanumeric, 8–30 chars)
TRACKING_PATTERN = re.compile(r'\b[A-Z0-9]{8,30}\b')
DATE_PATTERN = re.compile(r'\b\d{1,2}[\s/\-](?:Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec|\d{1,2})[\s/\-]\d{2,4}\b', re.IGNORECASE)


class NoFabricationGuard:
    """Accuracy guardrail: every fact in the response must exist in retrieved data."""

    async def check(self, context: dict) -> GuardrailResult:
        draft: str = context["response_draft"]
        retrieved: list[OrderStatus] = context["order_statuses"]

        # Build ground-truth sets from retrieved data
        valid_tracking = {o.tracking_number for o in retrieved if o.tracking_number}
        valid_statuses = {o.status for o in retrieved}
        valid_orders = {o.order_number for o in retrieved}

        # Extract candidates from draft
        draft_tracking = set(TRACKING_PATTERN.findall(draft))
        # Remove order numbers — those are expected
        unverified_tracking = draft_tracking - valid_tracking - valid_orders

        if unverified_tracking:
            return GuardrailResult(
                allowed=False,
                reason="Response contains tracking references not present in retrieved data",
                action="recompose",
                ungrounded_facts=list(unverified_tracking),
            )

        return GuardrailResult(
            allowed=True, reason="All facts in response grounded in retrieved data", action="proceed"
        )

implementation.name: HighValueCustomerGate
implementation.description: Routes the composed response draft to a CSR for review before sending if the customer is Tier 1 (top 50 accounts) or if any order in the response is delayed with no confirmed ETA. Protects key account relationships from unsupervised automated communication.
implementation.code:
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

from .models import ServiceCase, OrderStatus
from .clients import CSRQueueClient


@dataclass
class GuardrailResult:
    allowed: bool
    reason: str
    action: Literal["proceed", "hold_for_review"]
    review_queue: str | None = None


TIER_1_THRESHOLD = 1


class HighValueCustomerGate:
    """Service-quality guardrail: Tier 1 customers and delayed orders require CSR review."""

    def __init__(self, csr_queue: CSRQueueClient):
        self.queue = csr_queue

    async def check(self, context: dict) -> GuardrailResult:
        case: ServiceCase = context["service_case"]
        statuses: list[OrderStatus] = context["order_statuses"]

        is_tier1 = case.customer_tier == TIER_1_THRESHOLD
        delayed_no_eta = any(o.is_delayed and o.estimated_delivery is None for o in statuses)

        if is_tier1 or delayed_no_eta:
            reason = "Tier 1 customer" if is_tier1 else "Delayed order with no ETA"
            await self.queue.enqueue(
                case_id=case.id,
                draft=context["response_draft"],
                reason=reason,
            )
            return GuardrailResult(
                allowed=False,
                reason=f"{reason} — routing to CSR for review before dispatch",
                action="hold_for_review",
                review_queue="tier1_csr_queue",
            )

        return GuardrailResult(allowed=True, reason="Standard customer, no delays — auto-send approved", action="proceed")

implementation.name: EscalationRouter
implementation.description: Routes complaints, return requests, and low-confidence classifications to the human CSR queue at the Intake step, before any further autonomous processing occurs. Prevents the agent from attempting to handle request types that require human empathy and judgment.
implementation.code:
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

from .models import RequestType
from .clients import CSRQueueClient


@dataclass
class GuardrailResult:
    allowed: bool
    reason: str
    action: Literal["proceed", "escalate"]
    escalate_to: str | None = None


HUMAN_ONLY_TYPES = {RequestType.COMPLAINT, RequestType.RETURN, RequestType.TECHNICAL_SUPPORT}
CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD = 0.90


class EscalationRouter:
    """Service-quality guardrail: complaints and low-confidence requests go to human CSR."""

    def __init__(self, csr_queue: CSRQueueClient):
        self.queue = csr_queue

    async def check(self, context: dict) -> GuardrailResult:
        request_type: RequestType = RequestType(context["request_type"])
        confidence: float = context["classification_confidence"]

        needs_human = request_type in HUMAN_ONLY_TYPES or confidence < CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD

        if needs_human:
            reason = (
                f"Request type '{request_type.value}' requires human handling"
                if request_type in HUMAN_ONLY_TYPES
                else f"Classification confidence {confidence:.0%} below {CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD:.0%} threshold"
            )
            await self.queue.enqueue_case(context["case_id"], reason=reason)
            return GuardrailResult(
                allowed=False,
                reason=reason,
                action="escalate",
                escalate_to="human_csr_queue",
            )

        return GuardrailResult(
            allowed=True, reason="Routine status inquiry — proceeding autonomously", action="proceed"
        )

processes.name: Plan Supply Chain (SCOR P1)
processes.summary: Constraint-based supply chain planning: aggregate demand, assess supply capacity, balance supply/demand, publish plan — currently a 2-day manual cycle, target under 2 hours with 70% autonomous plan generation

feasibility_summary.risk_rationale: Bad plans propagate to all downstream processes — sourcing, manufacturing, and fulfillment all execute against the published plan. An incorrect supply plan can cause stockouts, excess inventory, or missed customer commitments.
feasibility_summary.autonomy_note: Routine plans are auto-generated and published; S&OP Manager reviews exception scenarios, capacity conflicts, and plans deviating more than 15% from baseline
feasibility_summary.complexity_rationale: APS/IBP APIs are mature and well-documented; main challenge is encoding business rules for constraint prioritization and exception thresholds

breakdown.note: Demand forecast data + statistical baseline + customer commit reconciliation
Capacity data across plants + supplier lead times + inventory positions
Constraint-based optimization reasoning + scenario comparison
Plan formatting + exception flagging + downstream notification generation

key_risks: Forecast errors propagate — agent cannot improve inherently poor demand signals
Constraint encoding gaps — business rules not captured in system may be missed
Exception threshold tuning — too tight creates noise, too loose misses real problems
Downstream dependency — all supply chain processes execute against the published plan
key_benefits: 95% cycle time reduction (2 days → <2 hours) enabling more frequent planning runs
Consistent constraint application — no manual prioritization errors or overlooked rules
Scenario analysis in minutes — agent can evaluate 10+ scenarios vs. 1–2 manually
Early warning of capacity conflicts surfaced automatically rather than discovered late
Planner time freed for strategic S&OP decisions rather than data aggregation

current_timeline.description: Demand Planner pulls statistical forecast from IBP, reconciles with customer commitments and sales inputs, manually adjusts for known events — error-prone spreadsheet consolidation
Supply Chain Planner checks plant capacity, supplier lead times, and current inventory positions across ERP and WMS — data scattered across multiple systems
S&OP Manager leads cross-functional review to resolve conflicts between demand requirements and supply constraints — largely manual negotiation and spreadsheet iteration
Planner updates IBP with approved plan, manually communicates to procurement, manufacturing, and logistics — no automated downstream notification

pain_points.title: Data Aggregation Bottleneck
pain_points.description: Planners spend 60% of cycle time gathering data from IBP, ERP, WMS, and spreadsheets rather than making decisions — high effort, low value-add

pain_points.title: Infrequent Planning Cycles
pain_points.description: Manual cycle time limits planning to weekly runs — demand signals and supply disruptions that occur mid-week are not incorporated until the next cycle

pain_points.title: Inconsistent Constraint Application
pain_points.description: Different planners apply capacity and supplier constraints differently — results vary based on individual knowledge and judgment

pain_points.title: Late Conflict Discovery
pain_points.description: Supply/demand imbalances often surface in S&OP meeting rather than earlier in the cycle, reducing time available to find solutions

agent_steps.name: Aggregate Requirements (P1.1)
agent_steps.autonomy_description: Fully autonomous — retrieves statistical forecast, reconciles customer commitments, and produces an aggregated demand signal. Demand Planner reviews output but no approval needed before passing to next step.
agent_steps.summary: Pulls statistical demand baseline from APS/IBP, reconciles with confirmed customer orders from ERP, incorporates any planner overrides flagged in the system, and produces a time-phased aggregated demand signal by SKU and location for the planning horizon.

context_data.description: ML-generated statistical baseline forecast by SKU, location, and time bucket for the planning horizon
Confirmed customer orders and delivery commitments that override statistical forecast for near-term buckets
Short-term demand sensing from POS/ship data to adjust near-term buckets
Demand Planner's documented overrides from previous cycle — promotions, product launches, known market events

skills.name: Forecast Reconciliation
skills.description: Merges statistical baseline with customer commits and planner overrides using time-bucket precedence rules — near-term uses confirmed orders, mid-term uses sensing + overrides, far-term uses statistical baseline

skills.name: Demand Signal Quality Check
skills.description: Scores forecast accuracy by SKU using historical MAPE, flags SKUs with poor forecast accuracy or anomalous demand patterns for Demand Planner review

skills.name: Aggregation
skills.description: Rolls up SKU-level demand to planning group, location, and time-bucket levels as required by the planning model in IBP

tools_and_data.purpose: Read statistical forecast and write aggregated demand signal
Read confirmed customer orders and delivery schedules
Read short-term demand sensing signal

guardrails.description: If aggregated demand deviates more than 20% from prior cycle at planning group level, flag for Demand Planner review before proceeding
Confirmed customer orders always override statistical forecast in near-term buckets — never reduce a customer commitment without human approval

agent_steps.decision_logic_mermaid:
graph TD
  A["Pull statistical\nforecast"] --> B["Load customer\norder commits"]
  B --> C["Apply planner\noverrides"]
  C --> D["Merge into\ndemand signal"]
  D --> E{"Deviation >20%\nvs prior cycle?"}
  E -->|Yes| F["Flag for\nDemand Planner"]
  E -->|No| G["Aggregate by\nSKU/location"]
  F --> H["Demand Planner\nreviews"]
  H --> G
  G --> I["Write demand\nsignal to IBP"]
  classDef decision fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#92400e
  classDef action fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#2563eb,color:#1e40af
  classDef human fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#5b21b6
  class E decision
  class A,B,C,D,G,I action
  class F,H human

outputs.name: Aggregated Demand Signal
outputs.description: Time-phased demand by SKU, location, and planning bucket — loaded into IBP as input to resource assessment

outputs.name: Demand Quality Report
outputs.description: MAPE by SKU, flagged anomalies, override summary — for Demand Planner awareness

involved_roles.involvement: Reviews demand quality report and approves any flagged deviations before cycle proceeds

agent_steps.name: Assess Resources (P1.2)
agent_steps.autonomy_description: Fully autonomous — queries all supply resource data and computes available capacity, supplier lead times, and inventory coverage. No human approval needed as this step only reads and assesses, producing a factual resource view.
agent_steps.summary: Queries ERP for plant capacity calendars and routing data, WMS for current inventory positions, and procurement data for supplier lead times and open POs. Computes available-to-plan capacity by resource and time bucket, inventory coverage days by SKU, and supplier delivery reliability scores.

context_data.description: Plant capacity calendars, work center rates, routing BOMs — defines available production capacity by resource and time period
On-hand stock by SKU and location, quality holds, lot expiry — available inventory after reservations
Confirmed supplier POs with expected receipt dates — supply already committed and in pipeline
Historical supplier lead times, on-time delivery rates, and current capacity constraints communicated by suppliers

skills.name: Capacity Computation
skills.description: Calculates available capacity per work center per time bucket after deducting planned maintenance, confirmed production orders, and safety buffers

skills.name: Inventory Coverage Analysis
skills.description: Computes days-of-supply by SKU and location, identifies items below safety stock, and projects inventory trajectory given current demand signal

skills.name: Supply Pipeline Consolidation
skills.description: Merges open POs, in-transit shipments, and production orders into a unified supply pipeline view by SKU and expected receipt date

tools_and_data.purpose: Read plant capacity calendars, work center data, routing
Read real-time inventory positions by SKU and location
Write resource assessment results as planning constraints

guardrails.description: Resource view must never show capacity available beyond the defined maximum utilization rate (default 85%) — safety buffer is non-negotiable
If last WMS cycle count for a location is >7 days old, flag inventory data as potentially stale before using in planning

  C --> D["Query open\nPOs & pipeline"]

outputs.name: Resource Availability View
outputs.description: Available capacity by work center and time bucket, inventory coverage by SKU, supply pipeline by receipt date — loaded as constraints into IBP

agent_steps.name: Balance Supply & Demand (P1.3)
agent_steps.autonomy_description: Runs constraint-based optimization autonomously; S&OP Manager reviews and approves when plan deviates more than 15% from baseline, when demand exceeds capacity by more than 10%, or when customer service level would drop below threshold.
agent_steps.summary: Runs constraint-based supply/demand balancing in IBP using the demand signal and resource constraints from previous steps. Generates a feasible supply plan, identifies gaps where demand cannot be met, evaluates alternative scenarios (expedite, substitute, defer), and recommends the optimal plan. Flags exception scenarios for S&OP Manager review.

context_data.description: Aggregated demand and resource availability loaded in steps 1 and 2 — primary inputs to optimization
Customer priority tiers, product priority rankings, minimum order quantities, and safety stock policies — constraint parameters for optimization
Last published plan used to measure deviation and detect significant changes requiring human review

skills.name: Constraint-Based Optimization
skills.description: Solves supply/demand balancing problem within capacity, inventory, and supplier constraints — minimizes stockouts for priority customers while respecting resource limits

skills.name: Scenario Generation
skills.description: Generates and evaluates alternative scenarios: expedite supplier, increase overtime, substitute SKU, defer low-priority orders — presents cost/service trade-offs

skills.name: Exception Identification
skills.description: Compares balanced plan against demand to identify uncoverable gaps, customer service level impacts, and deviations from prior cycle exceeding threshold

tools_and_data.purpose: Run constraint-based supply/demand balancing
Read business rules, customer priority data

guardrails.description: If balanced plan deviates >15% from prior cycle at planning group level, or if projected customer service level drops below 95%, S&OP Manager must approve before publishing
Tier 1 customer demand must be fully covered before allocating supply to lower tiers — priority rules are non-negotiable and not subject to optimization override
Balanced plan must be mathematically feasible — supply >= demand for priority commitments, no negative inventory positions, capacity utilization <= 100%

agent_steps.decision_logic_mermaid:
graph TD
  A["Load demand +\nresource constraints"] --> B["Run IBP\noptimizer"]
  B --> C{"Feasible\nplan found?"}
  C -->|No| D["Generate exception\nscenarios"]
  D --> E["S&OP Manager\nselects scenario"]
  E --> B
  C -->|Yes| F{"Deviation >15%\nor CSL <95%?"}
  F -->|Yes| G["Flag for S&OP\nreview"]
  G --> H["S&OP Manager\napproves"]
  H --> I["Finalize\nbalanced plan"]
  F -->|No| I
  I --> J["Pass to\nPublish Agent"]
  classDef decision fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#92400e
  classDef action fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#2563eb,color:#1e40af

  class A,B,D,I,J action
  class E,G,H human

outputs.name: Balanced Supply Plan
outputs.description: Feasible supply plan by SKU/location/time bucket — approved for publication to downstream processes

outputs.name: Exception Report
outputs.description: Uncoverable demand gaps, customer service level impacts, and scenario trade-offs presented to S&OP Manager

involved_roles.involvement: Reviews exception scenarios, selects preferred scenario when demand exceeds capacity, approves plan deviating >15% from baseline

agent_steps.name: Publish Plan (P1.4)
agent_steps.autonomy_description: Publishes autonomously once S&OP Manager or Supply Chain Planner has approved the balanced plan. Plan approval is the human gate; publication and downstream notification are fully automated.
agent_steps.summary: Takes the approved balanced plan and publishes it to all downstream execution processes: procurement (planned orders), manufacturing (production schedule), and logistics (delivery plan). Sends automated notifications with plan summaries to relevant stakeholders. Archives prior plan version for audit trail.

context_data.description: The approved constrained supply plan from step 3 — authoritative source for downstream execution
Procurement, production scheduling, and logistics planning interfaces that receive the published plan

skills.name: Plan Distribution
skills.description: Transforms IBP plan output into format required by each downstream system — planned POs for procurement, production orders for manufacturing, delivery schedule for logistics

skills.name: Stakeholder Notification
skills.description: Generates concise plan summary emails with key metrics (coverage rate, exceptions, changes vs prior cycle) for Supply Chain Planner, Demand Planner, and S&OP Manager

skills.name: Plan Versioning
skills.description: Archives the prior plan and tags the new plan with version, approval metadata, and planning cycle date for audit trail

tools_and_data.purpose: Read approved plan and mark as published
Write planned orders and production schedules downstream

guardrails.description: Plan cannot be published without explicit approval record from S&OP Manager or delegated Supply Chain Planner — approval timestamp and approver ID logged
Prior plan version must be archived before overwriting — enables rollback and audit trail for regulatory and internal review

  B -->|No| C["Block publish\nrequest approval"]
  B -->|Yes| D["Archive\nprior plan"]

  E --> F["Push to ERP\nprocurement"]
  F --> G["Push to ERP\nproduction"]
  G --> H["Push to\nlogistics"]

outputs.name: Published Supply Plan
outputs.description: Planned procurement orders, production orders, and delivery schedules loaded into ERP for execution by downstream processes

outputs.name: Plan Summary Notification
outputs.description: Key plan metrics and changes vs prior cycle sent to Supply Chain Planner, Demand Planner, and S&OP Manager

involved_roles.involvement: Approves plan for publication if within normal deviation thresholds; receives plan summary notification

orchestration.description: Four agents execute sequentially in a pipeline triggered by the planning cycle schedule (weekly or ad-hoc). Each agent produces structured outputs that feed directly into the next agent as IBP planning data. Human oversight is concentrated at two points: Demand Planner validates demand quality after step 1, and S&OP Manager reviews exceptions after step 3. Publication is gated on approval.

handoffs.trigger: Aggregated demand signal written to IBP
handoffs.payload: Time-phased demand by SKU/location/bucket, quality flags, override summary

handoffs.trigger: Resource availability view written to IBP constraints
handoffs.payload: Available capacity by work center/bucket, inventory coverage, supply pipeline

handoffs.trigger: Balanced plan approved (by agent or S&OP Manager)
handoffs.payload: Approved plan version ID, approval metadata, exception summary

shared_state.name: Supply Plan Lifecycle
shared_state.description: A Supply Plan object in IBP is progressively built through the four planning steps. It starts as an unconstrained demand signal and ends as an approved, published constrained supply plan distributed to all downstream processes.

stages.fields_added: Time-phased demand signal, quality scores, override log
Capacity constraints, inventory positions, supply pipeline
Constrained plan, exception flags, scenario alternatives, approval status
Publication timestamp, downstream distribution log, stakeholder notifications

guardrails_framework.description: Supply chain planning guardrails protect against plan errors that propagate to all downstream execution processes. Four priority levels address data integrity, financial exposure, operational feasibility, and regulatory/contractual compliance.

levels.label: Data Integrity
levels.description: Ensures planning inputs are accurate and current before optimization runs. Garbage-in plans produce garbage-out execution.
rules: Demand deviation >20% from prior cycle triggers Demand Planner review before proceeding
Inventory data older than 7 days flagged as potentially stale
Customer order commits take precedence over statistical forecast in near-term buckets
Plan version archived before overwriting — enables rollback

levels.label: Financial Controls
levels.description: Prevents plan outputs from creating uncontrolled financial commitments or write-off risk.
rules: Planned procurement orders above financial threshold require Buyer approval before ERP release
Excess inventory projections above policy level flagged for S&OP review
No plan may create planned orders beyond supplier-agreed capacity without Buyer confirmation

levels.label: Operational Feasibility
levels.description: Ensures the published plan is executable by downstream processes — no phantom capacity or impossible schedules.
rules: Capacity utilization capped at 85% maximum in plan — safety buffer enforced
S&OP Manager approval required when customer service level projection drops below 95%
Plan deviation >15% from prior cycle triggers review before publication
Balanced plan must pass feasibility check — no negative inventory, no capacity overrun

levels.label: Contractual Compliance
levels.description: Ensures the plan honors customer contractual commitments and supplier agreements.
rules: Tier 1 customer demand fully covered before allocating supply to lower tiers
Plan approval recorded with approver ID and timestamp for audit trail
Supplier minimum order quantities and lead times respected in planned orders
Published plan archived for 3 years per supply chain audit requirements

human_gates.trigger: Aggregated demand deviates >20% from prior cycle at planning group level
human_gates.description: Demand Planner reviews and confirms the demand signal before resource assessment proceeds

human_gates.trigger: Demand exceeds capacity or no feasible plan exists within constraints
human_gates.description: S&OP Manager selects preferred scenario from agent-generated alternatives

human_gates.trigger: Balanced plan deviates >15% from prior cycle or projected CSL <95%
human_gates.description: S&OP Manager reviews and approves plan before it can be published

human_gates.trigger: Before every plan publication
human_gates.description: Supply Chain Planner explicitly approves plan for publication to downstream systems

processes.name: Direct Procurement (SCOR S1/S2)
processes.summary: PO-to-payment automation: schedule deliveries, receive goods, verify quality, transfer to warehouse, authorize payment — currently 15–20 days manual cycle, target 5–7 days with 60% autonomous handling

feasibility_summary.risk_rationale: Financial risk from wrong payments and compliance risk from supplier sanctions screening. PO management and invoice matching are highly automatable; physical receiving and quality inspection require human presence.
feasibility_summary.autonomy_note: PO release, delivery scheduling, and invoice matching are fully autonomous; physical goods receipt and quality inspection require warehouse and quality teams; payment authorization above threshold needs Accounts Payable approval
feasibility_summary.complexity_rationale: ERP and supplier portal APIs are mature; invoice matching via OCR + AI is proven technology; main complexity is supplier portal diversity and three-way match edge cases

breakdown.note: PO data + supplier lead time + delivery window negotiation logic
GRN creation from scan data — minimal LLM, mostly structured processing
Quality spec comparison + inspection result interpretation + NCR drafting
WMS location assignment — mostly rule-based
Three-way match reasoning + invoice anomaly detection + payment instruction generation

key_risks: Wrong payment — three-way match errors can release payment for wrong quantities or prices
Supplier sanctions — payments to sanctioned entities; must screen every supplier before payment
Physical receipt — agent cannot verify physical goods condition; warehouse team always required
Quality disposition — agent can flag non-conformances but cannot make accept/reject decisions for safety-critical parts
key_benefits: 65% PO-to-payment cycle reduction (15–20 days → 5–7 days)
Invoice matching automation eliminates manual 3-way match for standard POs
Early payment discounts captured — faster processing enables dynamic discounting
Real-time GRN creation reduces goods receipt processing backlog
Consistent sanctions screening on every transaction regardless of Buyer workload

  end

process_overview.name: Direct Procurement (SCOR S1/S2)
process_overview.description: End-to-end direct procurement from planned order release through payment authorization, mapping to SCOR S1 (source stocked product) and S2 (source make-to-order product) steps S1.1–S1.5: schedule product deliveries, receive product, verify product, transfer product, and authorize supplier payment. Currently a 15–20 day manual process with Buyers managing supplier relationships, warehouse teams receiving goods, quality technicians inspecting, and AP executing payment runs. The target state automates PO management, delivery scheduling, invoice matching, and payment instruction generation while keeping humans in place for physical activities and financial approvals.
process_overview.mermaid:
graph LR
  subgraph Buyer["Buyer"]
    direction LR
    b1["Convert PO"]:::lane1 --> b2["Get Approval"]:::lane1 --> b3["Transmit to Supplier"]:::lane1 --> b4["Monitor Delivery"]:::lane1 --> b5["Resolve Discrepancies"]:::lane1
  end
  subgraph WH["Warehouse"]
    direction LR
    w1["Receive Goods"]:::lane2 --> w2["Count & Scan"]:::lane2 --> w3["Post GR"]:::lane2 --> w4["Put Away"]:::lane2
  end
  subgraph QA["Quality"]
    direction LR
    q1["Inspect"]:::lane3 --> q2["Accept / Reject"]:::lane3
  end
  subgraph AP["Accounts Payable"]
    direction LR
    a1["Register Invoice"]:::lane4 --> a2["Three-way Match"]:::lane4 --> a3["Schedule Payment"]:::lane4
  end
  b3 --> w1

  q2 --> w3
  w4 --> a1
  a2 --> b5
